content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814


October 19, 1976


Mr. Raymond Pyke
Hesperia Resorter
P.O. Box 947
Hesperia, CA  92345

Dear Mr. Pyke:

Thank you for your letter of October 12, 1976, requesting information concerning the manner in which free food and beverages contributed to a candidate should be reported on campaign statements required to be filed in accordance with the Political Reform Act.

The law makes no distinction between state and local candidates, all "expenditures made at the behest of a candidate, committee or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration is received for making the expenditure" (Section 82015)must be reported.  Free food and beverages should be reported on the candidate's campaign statement as a non-monetary contribution to his/her campaign.  If the value of the non-monetary contribution is $500 or more, the donor is an "expenditure committee" (Section 82013(b)) and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.

For your information I have enclosed a copy of the Political Reform Act with the referred to sections marked.

Please let me know if I may be of additional assistance.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator

RSK:dw
Enc.

hesperia
RESORTER

714 244-9381
P.O. BOX 947
HESPERIA, CALIF. 92345

10-12-76

FPPC
Sacramento,
Gentlemen:

Please send us
information on how we report
the food and beverages provided
to candidates and not upon
or their statements. These are
local candidates

Very many thanks

Raymond Ayke
Publisher

sections.conclusion: Free food and beverages should be reported on the candidate's campaign statement as a non-monetary contribution to his/her campaign. If the value of the non-monetary contribution is $500 or more, the donor is an "expenditure committee" (Section 82013(b)) and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.
sections.question_synthetic: How should free food and beverages contributed to a candidate be reported on campaign statements under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free food and beverages must be reported as non-monetary contributions on campaign statements. Donors of non-monetary contributions valued at $500 or more qualify as expenditure committees and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers but contains clear question and answer content embedded in the body text. The document includes a follow-up handwritten note from the requester. Confidence reduced due to document formatting issues and partial text OCR quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should free food and beverages contributed to a candidate be reported on campaign statements under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: Free food and beverages should be reported on the candidate's campaign statement as a non-monetary contribution to his/her campaign. If the value of the non-monetary contribution is $500 or more, the donor is an "expenditure committee" (Section 82013(b)) and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Raymond Pyke's inquiry regarding the reporting of free food and beverages contributed to a candidate, clarifying that such items constitute non-monetary contributions that must be reported on campaign statements.
